section.title: The Springtime Invitation
section.sectionRationale: The bride hears her beloved coming, leaping over the hills like a young stag, and he calls her out into the new world of spring: the winter is past, the flowers have appeared, arise and come away. This movement is one of the most joyful in the Song, a picture of love that summons the beloved into life and openness, guarded by a shared care to keep the little foxes from spoiling the vineyard.
section.thesis: Love comes seeking, calls the beloved out of winter into springtime, and asks to be guarded from the small things that spoil it; the bride answers with the confession of mutual belonging, my beloved is mine and I am his.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the beloved’s approach, leaping over the mountains to the window; his springtime invitation, arise and come away, for the winter is past; and the shared guarding of the vineyard against the little foxes, sealed by the refrain of belonging.

verses.text: Listen! My {{dodi|beloved}} approaches. Look! Here he comes, leaping across the mountains, bounding over the hills.
My beloved is like a gazelle or a young stag. Look, he stands behind our wall, gazing through the windows, peering through the lattice.
My beloved calls to me, “Arise, my {{raya|darling}}. Come away with me, my beautiful one.
For now the winter is past; the rain is over and gone.
The flowers have appeared in the countryside; the season of singing has come, and the cooing of turtledoves is heard in our land.
The fig tree ripens its figs; the blossoming vines spread their fragrance. Arise, come away, my darling; come away with me, my beautiful one.”
O my dove in the clefts of the rock, in the crevices of the cliff, let me see your face, let me hear your voice; for your voice is sweet, and your countenance is lovely.
Catch for us the foxes, the little foxes that ruin the vineyards, for our vineyards are in bloom.
My {{dodi|beloved}} is mine and I am his; he pastures his flock among the lilies.
Before the day breaks and shadows flee, turn, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of Bether.

dodi.gloss: Beloved, the bride’s characteristic name for her man, my beloved, dodi. It recurs like a refrain through the Song and reaches its height in the confession of mutual belonging, my beloved is mine and I am his.

raya.gloss: My darling, literally my companion or dear friend, the bridegroom’s recurring name for the bride. Here it opens his springtime call: arise, my darling, and come away.

basic.context: The bride hears her beloved coming from far off, bounding over the hills like a young stag, and he arrives at the window and calls her out. Winter is over, he says, the flowers are up, the birds are singing, so arise and come away with me. The lovers agree to guard their blossoming vineyard from the little foxes that spoil it, and she sums up their love in a line the whole Song remembers: my beloved is mine and I am his.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a poem of invitation, and its power lies in the picture of love that comes seeking. On the plain level it is the joy of a man calling his beloved into the open delight of spring. Read within the whole Bible, it also carries the note of a greater voice that calls His people out of winter into life. Both readings honor the text; the second grows out of the first.
basic.originalLanguages: The two pet names carry the scene: the bride’s dodi, my beloved, who comes leaping over the hills, and his raya, my darling, whom he calls to arise. The invitation, arise and come away, is a summons out of hiding and into the shared, blossoming world of love.
basic.typology: Love that comes seeking and calls the beloved out of winter pictures the God who comes to His people and summons them into life. The little foxes that ruin the vineyard picture the small compromises that spoil love if they are not caught early.
basic.christ: The Bridegroom who leaps over the hills, stands at the window, and calls, arise and come away, echoes the Christ who seeks His people, calls them by name out of death’s winter, and bids them come to Him. His voice is the one that turns winter into spring.
basic.god: God is a seeking God, who comes to His people rather than waiting at a distance, and who calls them into joy and openness. He also cares about the vineyard, the small foxes matter to Him, for love is guarded in the details.
basic.crossReferences: John 10:3 to 4, the good Shepherd calls His own by name and they follow. Song 6:3 repeats the belonging refrain. 1 Corinthians 15:54 to 57, the winter of death is finally past in Christ.
basic.oneStory: The springtime call is love’s summons out of winter into life. It sings of a man calling his beloved, and it whispers of the God who comes seeking, calls His people out of death, and brings them into the endless spring of His presence.

context.literary: This unit is a self contained spring poem, framed by the beloved’s bounding approach and his gazelle like departure at 2:17. At its center stands the invitation of 2:10 to 13, one of the most lyrical passages in Scripture, followed by the shared charge to catch the foxes and the belonging refrain of 2:16.
context.historical: The imagery is that of the Palestinian spring after the winter rains: wildflowers, migrating turtledoves, budding fig and vine. Foxes were a real threat to blossoming vineyards, gnawing the tender shoots, a fitting image for what quietly spoils love.
context.canonical: The call, arise and come away, and the language of a beloved summoned out of hiding, resonate across Scripture with God’s repeated summons of His people from darkness to light, from death to life, gathered up in Christ’s calling of His own.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To sing love as invitation: the beloved who comes seeking, the summons out of winter into the shared joy of spring, and the mutual care that guards love from the small things that spoil it, sealed by the confession of belonging.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage mostly describes the beloved’s call and the lovers’ joy, but the charge to catch the little foxes carries an enduring wisdom: guard love from the small compromises before they ruin the vineyard.

debates.question: What kind of book is the Song: a celebration of married love, an allegory of Christ and the church, or both?

views.label: A celebration of covenant married love
views.case: The springtime invitation reads most naturally as a man calling his beloved into the open joy of love, with vivid, earthy images of spring; Scripture openly honors such delight, Proverbs 5 and Genesis 2.

views.label: An allegory of Christ and the church, or God and Israel
views.case: Much of church history heard in the seeking beloved and his call, arise and come away, the voice of God summoning Israel, or Christ calling His church out of winter into life, in step with Hosea 2 and Ephesians 5.

views.label: Both, the literal love as a God-given sign
views.case: The fullest reading keeps both: the poem truly celebrates the invitation of married love, and because marriage pictures Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31 to 32, the seeking, calling love here rightly points to the Bridegroom who calls His own.

debates.bearing: Holding both keeps the springtime joy earthy and real while letting it echo the greater call of Christ, so the passage neither becomes mere sentiment nor loses the gospel note of a God who comes seeking.

study.originalLanguages: The scene turns on the two refrained names: dodi, my beloved, the woman’s word for the man who comes bounding over the hills, and raya, my darling, the man’s word for the woman he calls to arise. His invitation, arise and come away, is a call out of hiding into openness and life. The movement ends on the great line of belonging, my beloved is mine and I am his, dodi li waani lo, which becomes the Song’s heartbeat and a fitting confession of covenant love, human and divine.

typology.type: The beloved who comes seeking and calls, arise and come away
typology.fulfillment: Christ who seeks His people and calls them by name out of death’s winter into life, John 10:3 to 4.

typology.type: The winter past and the spring come
typology.fulfillment: The new creation dawning in Christ, whose resurrection ends the long winter of death, 2 Corinthians 5:17.

typology.type: My beloved is mine and I am his
typology.fulfillment: The mutual belonging of Christ and His church, I am my beloved’s and he is mine, secured in the covenant of grace.

study.christ: The beloved who bounds over the hills, stands at the lattice, and calls, arise, my darling, and come away, pictures the Christ who seeks His people and summons them out of the winter of sin and death into the springtime of new life. His is the voice that the sheep know and follow; His resurrection is the winter finally past. And the confession my beloved is mine and I am his is the settled joy of every soul that belongs to Him, held fast in a love that will not let go.
study.god: God is shown here as a seeking God who comes to His people rather than waiting aloof, and who calls them into joy, openness, and life. He is glad in the good world of spring He has made, and He cares even about the little foxes, for He guards the love of His people down to the details. His summons is always toward life.

crossReferences.note: The good Shepherd calls His own sheep by name, and they follow His voice, as the beloved calls, come away.
In Christ the old has passed and the new has come, the winter past and spring arrived.
The belonging refrain returns, I am my beloved’s and my beloved is mine.
Marriage, and its love, is a sign of Christ and the church.

study.oneStory: The springtime invitation is love as a summons out of winter into life. On its face it is a man calling his beloved into the joy of spring; heard within the whole story, it carries the note of the God who comes seeking, who calls His people out of death, and who will bring His bride at last into the unending spring of His presence.

quiz.q: How is the beloved described as he approaches in 2:8 to 9?

options.text: Coming eagerly, leaping over the mountains like a gazelle or young stag, and standing at the window.
options.note: His approach is swift, glad, and seeking.

options.text: Arriving slowly and reluctantly, unsure whether to come.
options.note: The picture is the opposite: eager, bounding, seeking her out.

options.text: Waiting at a distance for her to come to him first.
options.note: He does pause and stand at the wall, gazing through the windows, yet he is not aloof; he comes bounding to seek her and call her out.

options.text: Hidden and hoping not to be seen.
options.note: He does stand behind the wall and gaze through the lattice, but he is not hiding; he calls to her openly.

quiz.q: What is the heart of the beloved’s invitation in 2:10 to 13?

options.text: The winter is past and spring has come, so arise, my darling, and come away with me.
options.note: It is a summons out of winter into the shared joy of spring.

options.text: A warning that hard times are coming and she should hide.
options.note: The reverse: winter is over and the season of singing has come.

options.text: A request that she stay indoors until he returns.
options.note: He does summon her in view of his coming, but he calls her out into the open to come away, not to stay indoors and wait.

options.text: A list of chores to finish before the harvest.
options.note: The passage does name the blossoming vines and ripening figs, yet it is a lyrical call into springtime love, not a work order.

quiz.q: What do the little foxes of 2:15 picture?

options.text: The small things that quietly spoil love if they are not caught early.
options.note: Foxes gnaw the tender shoots; small compromises ruin a blossoming vineyard.

options.text: Enemies who openly attack the marriage from outside.
options.note: Foxes are indeed a threat to the vineyard of love, but the point is the little foxes, the small and hidden spoilers caught early, not open assault from outside.

options.text: The lovers’ own children.
options.note: The foxes are spoilers of the vineyard, not a picture of children.

options.text: Nothing; it is merely decorative imagery.
options.note: The verse is vivid poetic imagery, yet it carries real wisdom about guarding love from small ruin, so it is not merely decorative.

quiz.q: What does the refrain my beloved is mine and I am his express?

options.text: The glad mutual belonging at the heart of covenant love.
options.note: It is the Song’s heartbeat, echoed again in 6:3.

options.text: That love is a matter of ownership and control.
options.note: The refrain does use the language of belonging, mine and his, but it means glad mutual belonging and delight, not ownership or control.

options.text: A temporary arrangement soon to end.
options.note: It does describe a bond of belonging, but a settled covenant one, not a passing tie soon to end.

options.text: The bride’s wish that she belonged to someone else.
options.note: It is her joyful confession of belonging to this beloved.

quiz.q: How does the seeking, calling beloved point to Christ?

options.text: He pictures Christ who comes seeking His people and calls them out of death’s winter into life.
options.note: The Bridegroom’s voice turns winter to spring, as Christ calls His own by name.

options.text: He teaches that God waits passively for people to find Him.
options.note: The scene does show a God who loves, but a God who comes seeking, as Christ seeks the lost, not one who waits passively.

options.text: He shows that only the strong and swift are loved by God.
options.note: The point is the beloved’s eager seeking, not the merit of the sought.

options.text: He has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: The scene truly celebrates human love in its own right, yet Scripture reads the Bridegroom’s seeking love as a sign of Christ and His church, so it does point beyond itself.

questions: The beloved comes eagerly, seeking his darling and calling her out. How does it move you to know that God is a seeking God who comes to you rather than waiting at a distance?
Arise and come away, he says, for the winter is past. Where do you need to hear Christ calling you out of a personal winter into new life this season?
The lovers agree to catch the little foxes before they spoil the vineyard. What small compromise or neglect is quietly gnawing at something good in your life or relationships?
My beloved is mine and I am his, she says with settled joy. What would it look like to rest more deeply in belonging to Christ rather than in proving yourself?
The beloved calls the bride his dove hiding in the cliffs, longing to see her face and hear her voice. How might God be inviting you out of hiding and into honest nearness with Him?

translationNotes.note: The season of singing can also be rendered the time of pruning; either way it marks the arrival of spring.
The little foxes that ruin the vineyards is proverbial for the small things that spoil a blossoming love.
The mountains of Bether may name a place or mean the rugged or cleft mountains; the sense is the beloved bounding away over the hills at daybreak.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Song of Solomon 2:8-17, chapter 2. Poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. Every em or en dash in the source was converted to a comma or clean break; none remain.
